claim_bullets.bullet_text: The SI system defines seven base quantities and their corresponding base units, including time (second), length (meter), mass (kilogram), electric current (ampere), temperature (kelvin), amount of substance (mole), and luminous intensity (candela). (raw/mit8_01scs22_chapter2.pdf p.1-13)
As of May 20, 2019, all base SI units are defined in terms of seven constants, such as the cesium hyperfine frequency, speed of light, and Planck constant. (raw/mit8_01scs22_chapter2.pdf p.1-13)
The second is defined by the cesium hyperfine frequency, which is exactly 9,192,631,770 Hz. (raw/mit8_01scs22_chapter2.pdf p.1-13)
The meter is defined by the speed of light in vacuum and the second, with the speed of light being exactly 299,792,458 m/s. (raw/mit8_01scs22_chapter2.pdf p.1-13)
The kilogram is defined by the Planck constant, which is exactly 6.626 070 15×10⁻³⁴ kg⋅m²⋅s⁻¹. (raw/mit8_01scs22_chapter2.pdf p.1-13)

source_record_text: Chapter 2 of MIT 8.01SC Physics I: Classical Mechanics covers the International System of Units (SI), dimensional analysis, and order-of-magnitude estimates (Fermi problems).
